evidence.quote: If the amount exceeds $10,000 the transfer requires Approver authorization before any money moves.
The SourceBank debits the source; the DestBank credits the destination; the AuditLog records the final outcome and settles back to the initiator.
If the SourceBank cannot complete the debit (insufficient funds), the transfer fails, no money moves, and the AuditLog still records and settles the rejection.

missing: Small (<=$10,000) branch still routes a SmallTransfer message to Approver, an extra hand-off the intent does not call for (Approver involvement is only implied for large transfers), though it is a notice rather than an authorization gate and does not block the flow

result.reconstructed_intent:
This protocol models a bank transfer request among five parties: the Initiator (customer requesting the transfer), the SourceBank (account holding the funds), the DestBank (receiving bank), an Approver (who reviews high-value transfers), and an AuditLog (which records the outcome and reports back to the customer). The Initiator starts by submitting a transfer amount to the SourceBank, which then decides how to handle it based on the amount and account status, branching into exactly one of three paths. If it's a large transfer, the SourceBank forwards it to the Approver for review while notifying both the DestBank and AuditLog that a large transfer is pending; the Approver then either authorizes it (triggering notifications to DestBank/AuditLog, an actual debit/credit movement of funds between banks, and a final settlement confirmation relayed to the Initiator through AuditLog) or denies it (triggering denial notifications and a settlement message back to the Initiator without moving funds). If it's a small transfer, no approval step is required — the SourceBank directly informs the Approver and DestBank, logs it, moves the funds, and settlement is reported to the Initiator. If the SourceBank finds insufficient funds, it notifies the Approver, DestBank, and AuditLog of the failure, and the Initiator is informed via a settlement message with no funds transferred. In every branch, the process always concludes with the AuditLog sending a settlement/status message back to the Initiator, guaranteeing the customer receives a final outcome regardless of which path was taken or whether the transfer succeeded, was denied, or failed for lack of funds.</reconstructed_intent>
</invoke>

evidence.quote: If the amount exceeds $10,000 the transfer requires Approver authorization before any money moves.
If the SourceBank cannot complete the debit (insufficient funds), the transfer fails, no money moves, and the AuditLog still records and settles the rejection.
The SourceBank debits the source; the DestBank credits the destination; the AuditLog records the final outcome and settles back to the initiator.

missing: The protocol's branch selection (Large/Small/NoFunds) is a local choice made by SourceBank; the $10,000 threshold and the insufficient-funds condition are not structurally enforced by the session type itself (session types cannot encode arithmetic value constraints), so faithfulness to the numeric threshold and funds-check relies on trusting SourceBank's implementation rather than the protocol structure.

The intent does not explicitly specify an approval-denied outcome; the protocol adds a Denied sub-branch that is a reasonable, non-contradictory elaboration (no money moves, AuditLog still settles) but is technically an addition beyond what the intent states.

evidence.quote: The Buyer releases payment only after the goods are received
the Seller releases the goods only after payment is made
FundEscrow(Double) from Buyer to Escrow;
ShipGoods(String) from Seller to Carrier;
DeliverGoods(String) from Carrier to Buyer;
ConfirmReceipt(String) from Buyer to Escrow;
ReleasePayment(Double) from Escrow to Seller;
SettlementComplete(String) from Escrow to Buyer;

result.reconstructed_intent: This protocol models an escrow-mediated trade transaction among four parties: a Buyer, a Seller, an Escrow agent, and a Carrier. The intended flow is strictly linear: the Buyer first deposits payment into escrow; the Escrow agent then confirms to the Seller that funds are secured; the Seller ships the goods by handing them to the Carrier; the Carrier delivers the goods to the Buyer; the Buyer confirms receipt back to the Escrow agent; the Escrow agent then releases payment to the Seller; and finally the Escrow agent notifies the Buyer that settlement is complete. Each step must happen in this exact order, with every party performing its single designated action only when it's their turn. There is no branching, retry, cancellation, or timeout logic defined anywhere in the protocol — it describes only the single successful (happy-path) sequence of the trade, with no explicit provision for what happens if a party fails to send its message (e.g., goods not shipped, payment not confirmed), despite the protocol's name suggesting deadlock risk is a concern.

evidence.quote: TripRequest(String) from User to Coordinator;
The Coordinator gathers a quote from each of the three booking agents.
If all three are available and the total is within budget, confirm every booking, charge payment, and tell the user the trip is booked.
If any leg is unavailable or the total exceeds budget, cancel every provisional booking, do not charge payment, and tell the user the trip could not be booked.
choice at Coordinator {

missing: Intent's availability signal is implicit: quotes are plain Double values with no explicit availability flag, so the Coordinator's branch decision is modeled abstractly rather than the intent's availability check being expressed in the message payloads.

evidence.quote: SettlementComplete(String) from Escrow to Buyer;
The Buyer releases payment only after the goods are received; the Seller releases the goods only after payment is made.
The trade is complete once the goods are delivered and the payment is released to the Seller.

result.reconstructed_intent: This protocol coordinates a trip-booking workflow among six parties: a User, a central Coordinator, and three service providers (Flight, Hotel, Car), plus a Payment handler. The User submits a trip request to the Coordinator, who then asks each of the Flight, Hotel, and Car providers for a price quote and collects a quote back from each. After gathering all three quotes, the Coordinator alone decides whether to proceed or abandon the trip. On success, the Coordinator notifies all three providers to confirm their bookings, then instructs Payment to charge the total cost and waits for payment confirmation before reporting the final trip result to the User. On failure/cancellation, the Coordinator instead tells all three providers to cancel their reservations, tells Payment to skip charging (no money changes hands), and still reports a trip result back to the User. Either path always ends with the User receiving a final outcome message, and the two paths are mutually exclusive alternatives chosen entirely by the Coordinator.

evidence.quote: If all three are available and the total is within budget, confirm every booking, charge payment, and tell the user the trip is booked. If any leg is unavailable or the total exceeds budget, cancel every provisional booking, do not charge payment, and tell the user the trip could not be booked.
The outcome must be all-or-nothing — never a partial booking.

missing: Protocol has no explicit unavailability signal from Flight/Hotel/Car (quotes are always a Double price), so the 'leg unavailable' trigger from the intent is not structurally represented — it relies on Coordinator's unmodeled internal logic to interpret quote values.

No acknowledgment/failure message from Flight/Hotel/Car for ConfirmBooking or CancelBooking, so the protocol assumes these always succeed; not required by the intent's stated scenarios but a minor robustness gap.
No PaymentFailed branch if ChargePayment does not succeed, though this is outside the intent's specified failure modes.

differences: Description A does not explicitly specify what happens when the Approver denies a large transfer; Description B has a detailed denial path with specific notifications
Description B explicitly requires notifying the Approver even for small transfers (no approval path); Description A leaves this unspecified
Description B introduces intermediate 'pending' notifications when transfer awaits Approver review; Description A has no pending-state communication
Description B specifies all parties notified in insufficient-funds scenario (Approver, DestBank, AuditLog); Description A only mentions AuditLog recording
Description B formally guarantees the process always concludes with settlement to Initiator; Description A implies this but does not state it as a structural guarantee
Description B uses 'exactly one of three paths' language formalizing the branching structure; Description A leaves branching implicit
A uses conditional constraints (deadlock-creating); B uses strict linear ordering (deadlock-avoiding)
A leaves Escrow and Carrier behavior unspecified; B fully specifies all four parties' actions
A implicitly relies on unspecified escrow coordination to resolve deadlock; B explicitly sequences actions to prevent it
A treats Escrow as passive fund holder; B treats Escrow as active coordinator with confirmation, release, and notification steps
B mandates a single deterministic linear sequence; A's constraints could theoretically allow multiple orderings
A silent on failure cases; B explicitly states it has no failure/retry/timeout/cancellation logic
Description A explicitly requires budget checking as a decision criterion; Description B omits budget entirely from its decision logic
Description B mandates waiting for payment confirmation before notifying the user; Description A does not explicitly require this ordering
Description A specifies decision criteria as 'all legs available AND within budget'; Description B vaguely states the Coordinator 'decides' without listing criteria
Description B models Payment as an explicit sixth party with instruction and confirmation steps; Description A treats payment abstractly
Description B specifies explicit protocol messages (notifies providers to confirm/cancel, instructs Payment); Description A describes outcomes without message flow details

evidence.quote: Transfer an amount of money from a source account to a destination account. If the amount exceeds $10,000 the transfer requires Approver authorization before any money moves. The SourceBank debits the source; the DestBank credits the destination; the AuditLog records the final outcome and settles back to the initiator.

global protocol TravelBooking(
    role User,
    role Coordinator,
    role Flight,
    role Hotel,
    role Car,
    role Payment
)

missing: No SourceBank, DestBank, AuditLog, or Approver role exists in the protocol at all.
No debit/credit/settlement messages for a money transfer are present; instead the protocol models a travel booking flow (flight/hotel/car quotes and confirmation).
No $10,000 threshold or conditional Approver-authorization branch exists.
No insufficient-funds/rejection path with AuditLog recording and settling back to the initiator is present.
The protocol is for an entirely unrelated domain (trip booking with Coordinator, Flight, Hotel, Car, Payment) and does not implement any part of the described money-transfer intent.

evidence.quote: Transfer an amount of money from a source account to a destination account.
